old_claim.note: Global Positioning System (GPS) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: The corrected node is scoped to Shakey-era indoor mobile robot navigation; GPS/satellite localization belongs on outdoor navigation applications, not as a direct prerequisite for the 1966-1972 Shakey capability.

source_shape.source_locator: CHM describes Shakey as a 1966-1972 mobile robot using cameras, touch sensors, remote computing, route finding, and waypoint planning, with no GPS or satellite-localization component.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports Shakey-era mobile navigation as sensing, route finding, planning, and remote-computer control rather than satellite positioning.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the node chronology is anchored to Shakey, retaining GPS would smuggle a later outdoor localization technology into an indoor research-robot navigation scope.

ontology_before: The graph treated GPS as a direct enabler for mobile robot navigation generally, even though the scoped first-known Shakey example navigated indoors without satellite positioning.
ontology_after: The graph keeps mobile robot navigation anchored to sensing, planning, route finding, and control; GPS remains available for later outdoor navigation nodes without being a direct prerequisite here.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-23-mobile-robot-navigation-scope keeps GPS absent as a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to outdoor GPS-guided autonomous vehicles rather than Shakey-era mobile robot navigation.
A source showed Shakey's route finding or planning depended on GPS or satellite localization.
A downstream application node required GPS and no longer had a direct or separate path to it.
short_reason: GPS is an application-specific outdoor localization technology, not a direct prerequisite for Shakey-era mobile robot navigation.
